coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted attention as promising supports for the realization of single-atomic catalysts (SACs). However, MOFs have significant limitations in terms of mass transfer and the formation of tri-phase interfaces owing to their large number of micropores. In this study, we introduce a one-pot synthesis of a Fe-N-C catalyst with hierarchically ordered mesoporous and macroporous structures via the inverse self-templating of a zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). A mixture solution of pyrrolic nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots and Fe precursor is coated onto the surface of the ZIF-8 particles. During carbonization, the hard shells of ZIF-8 with a coating layer were maintained and adhered to each other, whereas the soft core of the particles decomposed. Significantly, the SACs exhibited a honeycomb-like morphology, and the pore size of the SACs is proportional to the size of the ZIF-8 particles. Our SAC with a pore size of 206 nm showed a half-wave potential of 0.889 V for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in 0.1 M KOH solution, which is 21 mV higher than that of the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst. Remarkably, membrane electrode assemblies prepared using the SAC achieve a high current density of 1.31 A cm−2 at 0.4 V, primarily due to the advances in mass transport. In addition, the catalyst showed remarkable activity in an acidic medium, after accelerated durability tests, and even under methanol crossover conditions.

FE-SEM images of ZIF-8-x (top) and synthesized FeNC-x (bottom). (a) ZIF-8-10 and FeNC-10, (b) ZIF-8-20 and FeNC-20, (c) ZIF-8-30 and FeNC-30, and (d) ZIF-8-60 and FeNC-60. head.ce:title: Single atomic Fe-based honeycomb catalysts with hierarchically ordered meso- and macropore for the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted attention as promising supports for the realization of single-atomic catalysts (SACs). However, MOFs have significant limitations in terms of mass transfer and the formation of tri-phase interfaces owing to their large number of micropores. In this study, we introduce a one-pot synthesis of a Fe-N-C catalyst with hierarchically ordered mesoporous and macroporous structures via the inverse self-templating of a zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). A mixture solution of pyrrolic nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots and Fe precursor is coated onto the surface of the ZIF-8 particles. During carbonization, the hard shells of ZIF-8 with a coating layer were maintained and adhered to each other, whereas the soft core of the particles decomposed. Significantly, the SACs exhibited a honeycomb-like morphology, and the pore size of the SACs is proportional to the size of the ZIF-8 particles. Our SAC with a pore size of 206 nm showed a half-wave potential of 0.889 V for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in 0.1 M KOH solution, which is 21 mV higher than that of the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst. Remarkably, membrane electrode assemblies prepared using the SAC achieve a high current density of 1.31 A cm−2 at 0.4 V, primarily due to the advances in mass transport. In addition, the catalyst showed remarkable activity in an acidic medium, after accelerated durability tests, and even under methanol crossover conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode of low-temperature fuel cells requires highly active catalysts because of its sluggish reaction rate [1]. In particular, platinum group metals (PGMs) have been the most effective catalysts for the ORR [2–7]; however, their limitations, such as scarcity, high cost, and insufficient stability toward industrial standards, are critical issues to their commercialization and widespread use in fuel cells [4,8,9]. To overcome these limitations, many types of PGM-free or heteroatom-doped carbon catalysts have been studied to replace PGM catalysts [10–19]. Considering the activity and stability of these alternatives, they should be applied to anion-transferred environments because of their relatively low overpotential for ORR at high pH. Recently, various types of PGM-free catalysts for anion exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) have been suggested [20,21]. Among them, single-atomic catalysts (SACs), wherein, a single atom of transition metals is coordinated with nitrogen atoms (M–Nx, M = Fe, Co, etc.), have shown outstanding electrochemical performance compared to PGM-based catalysts [22–31]. According to theoretical and experimental studies, M−Nx moieties act as active sites because they have suitable adsorption and desorption energies for oxygen and its reaction intermediates [32,33]. In particular, the moieties should be well dispersed and confined in the carbon framework with high electrical conductivity [34]. Therefore, the morphology and structure of carbon materials should be controlled to enhance the formation of active sites.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are topological isomorphisms of zeolites that have a well-defined porous structure. In particular, zinc-based ZIF (ZIF-8), in which Zn2+ ions form a tetracoordinate with 2-methylimidazole, has received much attention as an excellent host for M−Nx because Zn is easily replaced by other transition metals for SACs, and the organic ligands simultaneously form highly conductive carbon support during pyrolysis [35]. Considering the existing atomic structure of transition metals in SACs, the numerous micropores in ZIF-8 could prevent the agglomeration of the transition metal to form particles, leading to an increase in the number of M−Nx sites compared to the other carbon supports [36]. However, active site-rich SACs have severe limitations in mass and charge transfer because the predominant microporous architectures that form a unique atomic structure have deteriorated the electrochemical performance [37,38].

To address this intrinsic issue, many studies have investigated the development of mesoporous or macroporous textures in SACs derived from ZIF via template or template-free approaches [39–42]. Qiao et al. synthesized a three-dimensional SAC via the formation of Fe-doped ZIF-8 on a polystyrene template and subsequent removal of the template [43]. The mesopores and macropores in the catalyst were well developed, resulting in an outstanding performance in the high-current–density region. However, the inevitable and complicated post-treatment required to remove the heterogeneous template could lower the performance and reproducibility of the catalyst. A representative template-free approach involves enlarging the micropores through the etching of toxic gases generated during pyrolysis. Chen et al. studied mesoporous Fe-N-C catalysts prepared by pyrolyzing a mixture of Fe precursor, ZIF-8, and melamine as a source of nitrogen dopant and simultaneous etching of gases such as NH3 [44]. Although the catalysts can be prepared via a one-pot process without post-treatment, the nonuniformity of the evolution of the gases and subsequent etching may lead to the formation of a heterogeneous porous structure. Therefore, a facile strategy is required to synthesize highly active and stable SACs that predominantly consist of mesopores and macropores.

In this study, we introduce a one-pot synthesis of a Fe-N-C catalyst with hierarchically ordered mesoporous and macroporous structures via the inverse self-templating of ZIF-8. Organic ligands in ZIF-8 particles are easily and partially decomposed during pyrolysis, resulting in the formation of microporous carbonaceous polyhedra. A mixed solution of pyrrolic nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (pNC) and Fe precursor was coated on the surface of each ZIF-8 particle to maintain the skeleton of ZIF-8, adhere to the skeleton of other ZIF-8, and simultaneously provide anchoring sites for Fe ions to form Fe-based SACs. The SACs exhibited a honeycomb-like morphology, and the pore size of the SACs was proportional to the size of ZIF-8. The SAC, synthesized in this study with an average pore size of 206 nm, showed the optimal ORR performance with a half-wave potential (E1/2) of 0.889 V in 0.1 M KOH solution, which is 21 mV higher than that of the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst, and a kinetic mass current (Jk at 0.85 V) of 4.47 mA/cm2. It is noteworthy that the membrane electrode assemblies prepared using our SAC are able to attain a high current density of 1.31 A/cm2 at 0.4 V, primarily due to the improvement in mass transport. In addition, the catalyst showed high ORR performance in acidic media and significant stability in accelerated durability tests and under methanol crossover conditions.

The ZIF-8 material was synthesized using a modified version of a previously reported method [45]. Zinc nitrate hydrate (assay above 98.0%, Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.) (3 g) was dissolved in different amounts of methanol (Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.) (10, 20, 30, and 60 ml) (solution A). In addition, 6 g of 2-methylimidazole (Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.) was dissolved in methanol in proportional amounts (20, 40, 60, and 120 ml) (solution B). Solutions A and B were sonicated separately for 1 h and then mixed in a vial by stirring at 300 rpm for 24 h. The mixture was then centrifuged and washed several times using methanol and ethanol (Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.). Finally, after drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h,the ZIF-8-x white powder (x represents the amount of solvent in solution A) was obtained.

70 ml of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, assay above 99.7%, Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.) was added to a three-necked flask with a reflux setup. Reflux treatment was performed at 170 °C for 10 h while injecting oxygen. A dark brown pNC solution was obtained [46]. Fe-pNC was obtained by dispersing 20 mg of iron(iii) nitrate anhydrous (FeCl3, Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.) in 1 ml of the synthesized pNC.

100 mg of ZIF-8-x and 0.2 ml of Fe-pNC (Fe 1.4 wt% compared to ZIF-8) were mixed in a vial and sonicated for 1 h with 0.4 ml of NMP. After drying the resulting mixture overnight at 80 °C, light brown Fe-pNC@ZIF-8-x powder was obtained. The powder was pyrolyzed in a box furnace at 1000 °C (5 °C/min) under a nitrogen atmosphere. After 3 h of holding, the samples were slowly cooled to room temperature to obtain a carbonized FeNC-x powder.

The morphologies of the materials were observed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; SU8010, Hitachi High-Tech, Japan). A Cs-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope (JEM ARM200F, JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of the material and analyze the microstructure of the catalyst using scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) technique at 200 kV with a lacey carbon grid. The elemental composition of the samples was confirmed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping. The Brunauer–Emmet–Teller (BET) surface area and pore volume were measured using a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 (Micromeritics Instrument Corp., USA) system with N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K, and pore-size distribution (PSD) and micro/meso/macropore volumes were determined using a non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) model of N2@77 on carbon slit pores [47,48]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TG 209 F3 Tarsus analyzer (Netzsch, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku, Korea) with Cu K α radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) was used to examine the crystalline structures of catalysts at a scan rate of 5° min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system.

The electrochemical properties of the catalysts were investigated using rotating disk electrode (RDE) and rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) methods on a three-electrode Autolab PGSTAT 302 N potentiostat (Metrohm Autolab, Netherlands). A Pt wire (or graphite), Ag/AgCl, and glassy carbon were used as the counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. The catalyst ink was prepared in the following manner: for 1 h, 10 mg of the catalyst was sonicated in 700 μL of tert-butyl alcohol (Daejung Chemicals & Metal Co.), 300 μL of deionized water, and 100 μL of Nafion (5 wt% Sigma Aldrich, USA). Thereafter, 8 μL of the ink solution was dropped on glassy carbon and dried with disk rotation. The ORR polarization curves were measured using linear sweep voltammetry in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH or 0.1 M HClO4 at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. Based on the Nernst equation, all measured potentials (vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode) were translated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale.

The long-term durability was measured by the accelerated durability test with continuous cycling of 10,000 cycles in the range of 0.6–1.0 V (vs RHE) in 0.1 M KOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane electrode assembly fabrication and single-cell test
ce:section.ce:para: A membrane electrode assembly (MEA) was fabricated by implementing a gas diffusion electrode (GDE) using an electrospray method. Carbon paper (SGL 39BB, Sigracet) was used as a gas diffusion layer (GDL). The anion exchange membrane (FAA-3–50, 50 μm, FuMA-Tech) and the fabricated GDE were pretreated with a 1.0 M KOH solution for 24 h to convert to a hydroxide form and then washed with distilled water. The ink slurry used for electrospray was prepared as follows. The catalyst and ionomer (FAA-3-SOLUT-10, 10 wt% anion exchange ionomer solution in NMP, FuMA-Tech) were dissolved in isopropanol (Daejung) by sonicating for 1 h. As a catalyst, commercial PtRu/C (047379.06, Pt 40 wt% and Ru 20 wt%, Thermo Fisher) was used at a loading amount of 0.4 mg/cm2 on the anode, and FeNC was applied as a cathodic catalyst with loading amount in the range of 1.5 to 2.5 mg/cm2. A single cell was assembled by stacking the prepared membrane and GDE, and the hot press process was not performed. Single-cell test was performed by injecting fully humidified H2 at the anode and O2 at the cathode at 60 °C and maintaining a back pressure of 1.0 bar for measurements. The flow rate of hydrogen/oxygen was 0.8/1.0 L/min and the operating cell temperature was maintained at 60 °C. When cell performance stabilized under these conditions, single-cell polarization curves were obtained at a scan rate of 50 mA/s.

Fe-N-C catalysts with meso- and macroporous structures similar to that of a honeycomb were synthesized using a facile process, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). ZIF-8 particles were first wrapped using a mixture solution of Fe precursor and pNC (Fe-pNC@ZIF-8) and then thermally treated to form honeycomb-like porous Fe-N-C catalysts (hp-FeNC) at 1,000 °C and N2 atmosphere (5 °C/min, holding duration = 3 h). ZIF-8 particles synthesized via wet chemical approaches have a dodecahedral shape with sharp edges and a diameter of approximately 450 nm. The Fe-pNC@ZIF-8 particles were slightly larger (approximately 470 nm), had blunt edges, and even adhered to each other because of the coating effect of the Fe-pNC mixture solution. Fe ions on ZIF-8 can easily substitute for Zn atoms bonded with organic parts, leading to the formation of Fe–Nx active sites and simultaneous evaporation of Zn metal during thermal treatment. In our previous study, we applied pNC as an anchoring site to maximize the formation of single-atomic Fe-N-C catalysts through the pinning effect, wherein, the positively charged Fe ions are electrically attracted to the negative charge in the pyrrolic N of the pNC on the carbon support [46]. Further, the morphologies of the samples prepared via the carbonization of ZIF-8, pNC-coated ZIF-8, and Fe precursor-coated ZIF-8 showed individually existing particles and a one-dimensional structure, as shown in Fig. S2. Significantly, we realized the formation of honeycomb-like porous Fe-N-C catalysts through the precise control of the amounts of pNC and Fe precursor. In this study, the catalysts were prepared using 26.0 wt% pNC and 1.4 wt% Fe precursor (vs. weight of ZIF-8), which are three times higher compared to those used in previous studies [46]. The catalysts had a honeycomb-like structure comprising macropores of approximately 200 nm on the surface and inside the carbon, as shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b).

Considering that the honeycomb-like porous structure was formed after carbonization, investigating the decomposition properties of pNC, Fe precursor, and ZIF-8 as well as their mixture is essential. TGA was conducted under the same conditions as carbonization, and the thermograms are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The decomposition temperatures of individually existing Fe precursor, pNC, and ZIF began at approximately 90, 150, and 580 °C, respectively; their weights were almost stable till approximately 450, 500, and 870 °C, respectively, indicating their sequential decomposition. The mass loss of Fe-pNC@ZIF-8 up to 500 °C is attributed to the Fe precursor and pNC. However, the mass loss of Fe-pNC@ZIF-8 above 500 °C occurred in two steps and at relatively high temperatures, unlike that of ZIF-8. The Fe precursors and pNC would have initially converted into Fe metal and carbon, respectively, affecting the decomposition of ZIF-8 and the formation of a honeycomb-like structure.

The effect of sequential decomposition properties on the structure of the catalysts was investigated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and STEM. Wavy and short graphene layers are observed in the catalysts, as shown in Fig. 2(b). In addition, well-ordered graphitic carbon with an interplanar spacing of approximately 0.35 nm was observed. In particular, metal nanoparticles with diameters of 20–50 nm were rarely present around graphitic carbon (Fig. 2(c)). From the SAED pattern, the nanoparticles were Fe carbides with a d-spacing of 0.223 nm (Fig. S3). Most Fe metal, except for a few Fe carbide particles, exists in atomic dispersion coordinated with nitrogen atoms by a substitution reaction with the Zn atom in ZIF-8 (Fig. 1 (c)–(e)). We additionally conducted extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) analysis to confirm the presence of Fe single atomic sites in our catalyst. Fig. S4 shows the radial distribution function calculated from Fourier-transformed (FT) EXAFS spectra of the references (Fe foil and Fe phthalocyanine (FePc)) and our catalyst (hp-FeNC). The catalyst and FePc exhibited distinctive EXAFS features, notably different from those observed in the Fe foil. The former mainly comprised FeN/O bonds at around 1.43 Å, while the latter contained only FeFe bonds. Interestingly, the minor peaks at 2.19 and 2.50 Å, which were assigned by FeFe bonds and FeC bonds, respectively, were also observed in our catalysts. Fei et al. proposed that the coexistence of the main Gaussian-like peak (FeN/O bonds) and the minor satellite peak suggests the formation of the MNxCy atomic structure, where the satellite peak corresponds to the MetalC bond contribution [71]. Hence, it can be inferred that our catalyst consists of a significant presence of single atomic Fe-Nx sites, along with a minor presence of Fe–C configuration.

Considering the thermal decomposition results and morphological and structural properties, we propose a probable formation mechanism for honeycomb-like porous catalysts. First, the pNC on the surface of each ZIF-8 might be used as a protective layer, leading to the formation of a hard-shell@soft-core structure. During high-temperature treatment, the Fe precursor with electrostatic attraction to pNC is reduced to Fe atoms. Among them, few atoms grow into Fe particles, and most Fe atoms coordinate with nitrogen atoms to form single-atomic catalysts. The excess Fe nanoparticles and pNC might act as a catalyst and adhesive source, respectively, to enhance the crystallinity of the carbon structure and simultaneously attach the hard shell of other ZIF-8 to each other. The soft core may be simultaneously decomposed and converted into mesopores or macropores. Therefore, the honeycomb-like catalysts could be realized through the pore-forming reaction of the soft-core part and the networking reaction of the hard-shell part with the Fe particles and pNC.

Considering the probable mechanism, the pore size of the catalysts should depend on the size of the ZIF-8 particles. According to a previous study, the particle size can easily be controlled by the amount of solvent, owing to the change in the reaction rate and the number of nuclei [45,49]. ZIF-8-x (where x is the volume of the methanol solvent) particles were synthesized under the same conditions, such as the molar ratio of precursor to solvent, reaction time, and temperature, except for the amount of solvent. The particle size tended to decrease as the amount of solvent (x value in ZIF-8-x) increased, as shown in the insets of Fig. 3 (a)–(d). The average diameters of ZIF-8-10, ZIF-8-20, ZIF-8-30, and ZIF-8-60 are approximately 1203, 444, 254, and 98 nm, respectively. The morphology of the FeNC-x catalysts carbonized using the corresponding ZIF-8-x was observed using SEM (Fig. 3(a)–(d)). In the case of the FeNC-10 catalyst derived from the largest-sized ZIF-8, a honeycomb structure was not observed. It seems that the particles were entangled, melted, and agglomerated with each other (Fig. 3 (a)). In contrast, the FeNC-20, FeNC-30, and FeNC-60 catalysts exhibited honeycomb-like structures with average pore diameters of 206, 103, and 39 nm, respectively (Fig. 3(b)–(d)). In particular, considering low-magnification images of the catalysts, honeycomb-like porous structures with mesopores and macropores are formed throughout the catalysts depending on the particle size of ZIF-8-x (Fig. S5 and Fig. 3(e)).

Further, the microporous and mesoporous structures in the catalysts were analyzed via N2 physisorption. As shown in Fig. 3(f), all FeNC-x catalysts showed type-IV H4 with significant uptake at low p/p0 values due to micropores and a distinct hysteresis loop of mesopores, while NC derived from only ZIF-8 showed a type-I isotherm with minimal hysteresis (Fig. S6 (a)) [50]. FeNC-60 catalyst has a prominent hysteresis loop, indicating a mesoporous structure, which is consistent with the SEM results. However, the macropore-rich FeNC-20 and FeNC-30 catalysts exhibited a relatively smooth hysteresis loop owing to the measurement limitation of N2 physisorption for the macropores [51]. The pore size distribution (PSD) of the catalysts was calculated based on non-localized density functional theory (NLDFT), as shown in Fig. 3 (g). As summarized in Table S1, FeNC-20 shows the highest BET surface area of 835.4 m2/g, followed by FeNC-30, 10, and 60. Few studies have reported that the formation of pores in thermally treated ZIF-8 is dependent on the particle size owing to differences in thermal transport inside the particles [52,49]. In the case of small particles, the removal of solvents and uncarbonized organics related to pore formation might occur homogeneously. In contrast, the evolution of gases from relatively large particles might occur heterogeneously, leading to the heterogeneous formation of large pores due to the channeling effect. Therefore, we believe that there is an optimum pore size derived from the particle size in terms of mass transfer to maximize structural properties and electrochemical activity.

The effect of the particle size of ZIF-8 and the amount of pNC coating on the catalysts were investigated using XRD, and the corresponding diffraction patterns are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and S5, respectively. All catalysts exhibited a strong peak at approximately 26.4°, and several weak peaks at 40°–45° assigned to the carbon (002) plane and Fe particles, respectively [53]. The catalysts showed a sharper peak compared to that of NC. (Fig. S6(b)) The cubic Fe peak at 44.7° for the FeNC-10 catalyst was sharper than those of the catalysts, while the diffraction properties of the (002) plane for the catalysts were approximately the same. This indicates that larger iron metal particles existed in the FeNC-10 catalyst owing to pore collapse and Fe agglomeration. From the diffractograms of the prepared catalysts, the peak for the (002) plane becomes intense as the amount of pNC increases (Fig. S7). Considering the probable formation mechanism, the carbon-source-rich environment during metal-induced carbon crystallization might improve the crystallinity of the catalyst.

The chemical configuration of the catalysts was investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as shown in Fig. 4(b)–(d). The XPS survey profiles showed major peaks of C, N, and O and a minor peak of Fe at binding energies of 284, 400, 531, and 710 eV, respectively. In accordance with the XRD results, the peaks related to Zn completely disappeared at the pyrolysis temperature. The Fe content in the catalysts, as determined using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, was approximately 1.6 wt%, regardless of the catalyst. Even though the peak intensity of Fe 2p is relatively low owing to the low amount of Fe metal, a peak shift was observed, as shown in Fig. S8a). To discern the positive shift of the peaks, the Fe 2p spectrum was deconvoluted, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The Fe 2p spectrum consists of three groups with doublets at 707.6/720.7, 710.6/723.6, and 714.3/ 727.4 eV, assigned to the Fe0, Fe2+, and Fe3+ of 2p3/2/2p1/2, respectively [39,42]. All the catalysts exhibit major peaks of Fe2+, indicating that Fe coordinates with N. In particular, the Fe0 state (metal or carbide) tends to decrease, whereas the Fe3+ representing Fe-N5 exhibits the opposite trend with a decrease in the size of the ZIF-8 particles [54]. This might have originated from the external surface area of the ZIF-8 particles on which the negatively charged pNC was coated. In other words, in the case of large ZIF-8 particles with a relatively narrow external surface area, pNC was overcoated on the surface of ZIF-8, leading to the formation of Fe metal or carbide owing to a decrease in the number of adsorbed sites for Fe ions [49]. In contrast, well-dispersed Fe atoms exist as Fe2+/Fe3+–Nx moieties rather than as metal or carbide.

The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 4(d). The spectra could be well-fitted with four peaks, which were assigned to the pyridinic N (398.3 ± 0.1 eV), pyrrolic N (400.0 ± 0.1 eV), graphitic N (401.0 ± 0.1 eV), and oxidized N (403.3 ± 0.1 eV) [55]. Pyridinic-N affects the formation of the Fe–Nx structure and graphitic N has a positive impact on the transport of intermediates during the ORR [56,57]. The relative contents of the N configuration are shown in Fig. S8(b). The areal ratio of pyridinic N for the FeNC-30 and FeNC-60 catalysts is 44–46 %, which was approximately 3–5 % higher than that for FeNC-20, indicating that the number of Fe–Nx sites for the former was slightly higher. In FeNC-10 and FeNC-20 catalysts, the graphitic N content in the six-membered hexagonal structure was predominant, which is approximately 6–8% higher than that in others. Considering the metal-induced crystallization for the formation of N-doped carbon, the preferential formation of graphitic N in the FeNC-10 and FeNC-20 catalysts might originate from Fe metal or carbide [58].

The ORR performance of the catalysts was investigated using a rotating disk electrode (RDE) in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at room temperature. The FeNC-20 catalyst showed the highest activity, even when compared to the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and S9. Considering the pore structure of the catalysts with micropores, mesopores, and macropores with sizes above ∼100 nm, the FeNC-20 catalyst has sufficiently large pores and high surface area to facilitate the transfer of reactants and products because the pores were derived from the large ZIF-8-20 particles. In contrast, the FeNC-10 catalyst with collapsed pores showed values as low as 63 mV (E1/2) and 2.597 mA/cm2 (J at 0.85 V) compared to those of FeNC-20. Mesoporous FeNC-60 and macroporous FeNC-30, derived from particles smaller than ZIF-8-20, showed gradually improving activity. In particular, the limiting current density of the FeNC-20 catalysts was significantly high, indicating a mass transfer improvement related to the pore structure. The FeNC-20 catalyst demonstrates superior activity and durability compared to other reported non-platinum catalysts, as summarized in Table S3. This exceptional performance can be attributed to its efficient macroporous honeycomb structure, which facilitates vigorous mass transfer in the electrodes.

Fig. 5(b) shows the Tafel plots and slopes of the catalysts in the range of 0.86–0.96 V. The slopes of the FeNC-10, FeNC-20, FeNC-30, and FeNC-60 catalysts were 79.4, 62.1, 71.9, and 80.0 mV/dec, respectively. The FeNC-20 catalyst outperforms the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst in terms of kinetics and mass transfer, given that the latter had a Tafel slope of 71.1 mV/dec. Through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we confirmed the electron transfer properties of the catalysts (Fig. 5(c)). The FeNC-20 catalyst showed the smallest semicircle compared to the other catalysts. This could be attributed to the crystalline structure of carbon and kinetic improvement of catalysts that were partially and homogeneously induced by the Fe carbide and single atomic structure [59]. To determine the effect of Fe carbide on the ORR activity, acid leaching was performed. After treatment, the activity of the FeNC-20 catalyst decreased significantly (Fig. S10). Therefore, we believe that the FeNC-20 catalyst has active sites coexisting with atomic Fe–Nx and articulate Fe carbides, which could realize a synergetic effect on the ORR [22,53].

Furthermore, the durability of Pt/C catalysts is a major limitation. Under the experimental conditions of this study, the commercial 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst was easily deactivated, as shown in Fig. S11. After an accelerated deactivation test (ADT) over 10,000 cycles, the FeNC-20 catalyst was significantly stable exhibiting a decrease of 5 and 10 mV in terms of the onset potential and half-wave potential, respectively (Fig. 5(d)). An additional electrochemical aspect of the FeNC-20 catalyst was investigated as shown in Fig. S12. In a 0.1 M HClO4 electrolyte, the catalyst has an onset potential of 0.847 V and a half-wave potential of 0.787 V, indicating a small difference of −33 mV compared to those of 20 wt% Pt/C catalysts. The FeNC-20 catalyst also had electrochemical methanol tolerance characteristics, while the activity of the commercial catalyst significantly ameliorated when 2.0 M methanol was added. Therefore, the synthesized catalysts with honeycomb-like porous structures could be potential alternatives to various types of electrochemical electrodes.

Subsequently, the FeNC-20 catalyst with the best activity was further analyzed as a cathode electrode in AEMFC, with varying quantities of the catalyst loading in MEAs. Fig. S13 exhibits that the MEAs with 2.0 mg/cm2 of FeNC-20 catalyst had the most outstanding single-cell performance, while MEAs with 1.5 mg/cm2 and 2.5 mg/cm2 of the catalyst had relatively low performance due to the lack of active catalyst and the mass transport limitation of thick layers, respectively. In Fig. 6 (a), the MEA optimized using the FeNC-20 catalyst showed a current density of 0.84 A/cm2 at 0.6 V and a maximum power density of 0.59 W/cm2, comparable to that using a 40 wt% Pt/C catalyst, even though the MEA with FeNC-20 catalyst had a higher ohmic loss compared to that with the Pt/C catalyst due to the thicker electrode (Table S4). The maximum power density of the FeNC catalyst w/o a honeycomb structure (FeNC-pristine, Fig. S14) which has the same amount of iron loading as FeNC-20, was approximately 70 % of that of the FeNC-20 catalyst. Interestingly, the potential governed by the mass-transfer loss of the MEAs started at 0.85, 1.05, and 1.19 A/cm2 for FeNC w/o honeycomb structure, 40 wt% Pt/C, and FeNC-20 catalysts, respectively, meaning that reactants and products inside the FeNC-20 catalyst are transported well. Comparing those values from recently published papers [60–70], it is clear that our MEA with the single atomic Fe-based honeycomb catalyst exhibits remarkable performance, especially at high current densities, which are the mass-transfer control region (Fig. 6(b)). Therefore, it might be concluded that the formation of hierarchically ordered meso- and macropores within the catalysts could remarkably improve the mass transfer resistance of electrodes in the fuel cells and electrolyzers using MEAs, opening the way for a cleaner and more environmentally friendly hydrogen economy.

Honeycomb-like single-atomic Fe-N-C catalysts with mesopores and macropores were synthesized via the inverse self-templating of ZIF-8. The Fe precursor and pNC coated on the surface of the ZIF-8 particles played a role in maintaining the hard shells of the particles and adhering them to each other, leading to the formation of a honeycomb structure during carbonization. The mesopore and macropore sizes of the catalysts could be easily controlled by modifying the size of the ZIF-8 particles. All electrocatalysts had Fe–Nx moieties and Fe carbide particles, even though the proportions of both configurations varied as a function of the ZIF-8 size. The synthesized SAC, with an average pore size of 206 nm, showed high ORR performance in alkaline/acidic media, significant stability in accelerated durability tests and under methanol crossover conditions; therefore, our facile approach facilitating high electrochemical performance will enable the realization of a highly active and stable electrocatalyst for ORR under diverse and practical conditions.
